item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced, and thus is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting object that doesn't threaten the fabric of reality. +1 and I hope to read more like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh, I was wondering if that was the reason, having independently run the numbers. Fun stuff :) … maybe it was the making of a young reality bender who was only able to exert influence via imbuing familiar objects with certain powers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Explaining that part kills it for me a little, though - if you're actually still submerged and the water has merely become imperceptible, shouldn't the net effect of crouching down / bowing forwards so your head is below the surface (or indeed, standing in a deep enough pool that you only just have your nostrils poking into the "displaced" area) be, well… near immediate drowning?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless they're Doctor Bright. Monkey business and all. I can't imagine he wouldn't do something mischievous with it. Like leaving it in a small room room filled to 45 cm with water displaced to nowhere so it LOOKS empty and then when someone moves the duck their office is filled with water.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only thing that bothers me about this is: Where did the foundation get the duck? Where was it found?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The line "Four subjects of sufficient strength and flexibility […]" greatly confused me, and made me go back to re-read it all (without learning anything new) because I thought I'd missed some detail about the SCP's mass.

I suggest changing this to "Four physically-fit subjects […]"

Beyond that, thank you. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is incorrect. Wrinkly fingers are a neurological response to water, in order to maintain grip. This was verified by scientists; people who'd had the nerves to their fingers severed, their fingers didn't prune up, and they reported it was more difficult to maintain grip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You did notice that the author specifically confirmed this in the comments, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I figured out the purpose of this thing…

+ show block
– hide block

It's designed so that kids can get out of having a bath. Children can drain their bathwater using it, but since they get the "prune skin" effect from holding it for a long period, they can fool their parents into thinking they were in the bath when they weren't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: IT'S ALWAYS STEVE!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just because it's more suited for anomalous items doesn't mean it can't have its own article. I think the LoAI is more suited for anomalous items that you have no way of doing anything else with, other than a basic description and idea of what it does. This had testing and everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Um? This really seems like an anomalous item.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought even the hints might be too strong— but I'm glad it works, thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A couple points of vagueness - mentioning the item's origin, and its "intended" purpose. Neither are explained in any measure, and that's why I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ignore This Addendum:

Incident F-1992; in song.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Chimps tend to behave better than most little kids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hehe… I am very pleased that someone enjoyed that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted when I saw "anomalous duck". Superb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
^

152.4 x 81.3 x 45.7 cm, specifically is the loose metric conversion of 60"x30" and about 16-18" deep, which is the (now somewhat oldfashioned) "standard" bathtub size.

And, according to the youngest relatives of my acquaintance, it's the time wasting that's most objectionable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahhh, hence the waist-up dryness. Intriguing; it makes sense now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last note indicates that in "proper" usage, this SCP would enable a child to "take a bath", being submerged enough in the tub to satisfy parental requirements, without having to actually, well, bathe. It's not uncommon for very young children to desire to avoid bathing, for reasons which were always unclear to me (my own children can't wait to get wet on any occasion that presents itself).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very neat, though I have a quibble about saying "approximately" and then following it up with measurements to the tenth of a centimeter. Nice Tantalus reference, I very much appreciated that. The last note doesn't seem to add anything, or else I'm not reading it properly. The bit before it at least keeps this from being overly useful. Also, fixed a pair of typos for ya.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is adorable. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually feared it would bomb for crossing the whimsy line, personally. And it does work on bodily fluids, as the pruning vaguely implies, but only after very long periods of exposure. I was trying to hint that it operates more on perception than science, so the presence of water in other forms wouldn't necessarily "apply"….. and sometimes stuff can just get too pedantic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks… bizarre is a little more natural for me than horrifying, and I think it makes more sense to have an abundance of Safes vs Keter, even if there is no canon.

Pretty sure it's not Wondertainment— wasn't trademarked, and I think they have a pretty good idea of where it might have come from. 1992 was an interesting year. Plus, I like to think that this one has really only done harmful or dangerous things in the hands of adults, who use it in a way it isn't "meant" to be used (as opposed to the freaky earnest "an alien's idea of fun" I get from Wondertainment stuff). Sometimes the scientific method misses the obvious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like it should at least work on bodily fluids. But it doesn't. Execution is fine, but the idea seems… lacking. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, yes, actually… which became a dorky cartoon.

There was a scene where someone carried the containment box into a dining area, and it displaced all the sodas in the general area, but I thought that was dumb. They also tried it on a dog, and it had no effect. A living monkey was not available, and I'm actually a little unsure what would happen… when it comes down to it, though, the duck possibly operates on perception.

So really, I guess it would depend if it mistook a chimp for a little kid…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahaha, thanks… the doubt crossed my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent article. I was wondering if, in your testing, you considered the effect of a non-human holding it, such as a chimp? Just curious.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not a -J SCP and it has a rubber duck in it? And you pulled it through? Upvoted haaaaaard.
Good job, sir.

Cheers, D.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like that final note. Is this from Dr. Wondertainment, by any chance?

Either way, it's refreshing to see more items that are just plain weird and inexplicable, not imminent dangers to the existence of the very universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
aha, I'm glad!

Is there anything you'd suggest for the formatting? I'm still kind of wildly experimenting with what works/looks good…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks— and I agree with this rule, but only if it goes past one number after the decimal. I feel like 3.5 cm is still approximate, in some circumstances.

And more importantly, the dimensions were converted to metric and I kinda want to keep them specific for dumb bad joke reasons

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The formatting here seems a bit iffy, but it's a fine item. Nice and simple, with a decent amount of mystery remaining.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very simple, and very odd. I like it.

Only thing is, using "approximately x.yzwq" centimeters is an oxymoron. Try either removing the decimals or removing approximately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks!

… I try to keep the tests sane. I had some headcanon dialogue to the effect of

"well what about liquids that aren't water"
"like what"
"well like ketchup"
"…you're suggesting filling a pool with ketchup, for tests?"
"well…"
"steve you're fired"

running through my head, not gonna lie. I was actually worried that the note at the end about the "purpose" made the punchline to obvious, simple as it is, but I think I'll keep it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! ….this was basically my reasoning in writing it, I freely admit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simple, well-described, interesting effect, sane tests. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why, but this makes me happy. Maybe it's because of the simplicity of it. Of course, it might just be that I like rubber ducks. Upvoted.
